notes.type_correctness: All DB types (JobRow, EventRow, StatsRow, JobResult) are well-defined and match the SQLite schema. One `_db: any` in QueueManager could be typed as `DbInstance | null` from db.ts, but this is a minor improvement, not a correctness failure. One real type-contract bug fixed: srm.ts was parsing `{ processId }` from the spawn response but the server returns `{ jobId }` — log filtering silently broke in production.
notes.pattern_adherence: All SQL queries use parameterized statements (no string interpolation). Migration pattern is idiomatic (numbered migrations, schema_migrations tracking table). Shell spawn uses `shell: false` correctly.
notes.test_coverage: 95 tests across 5 test files. The web-manager integration test (srm.test.ts) used a mock that matched client expectation rather than server contract, masking the processId/jobId mismatch bug. Tests cover happy paths, error paths, and edge cases well, but the mock fidelity gap allowed a production bug through.
notes.security: No SQL injection — all queries are parameterized. No command injection risk beyond what `--dangerously-skip-permissions` already implies (which is exempted per design). The spawn endpoint accepts arbitrary command strings which are split on whitespace and passed to the claude binary — shell injection is not possible since shell:false is used. Server binds to 127.0.0.1 only.
notes.architectural_alignment: QueueManager cleanly absorbs the spawner logic. DB layer is well-separated with no circular dependencies. The client's hardcoded `ws://localhost:4200` is a design limitation: the client is development-only (no static serving in Express), but this should be noted for future production deployment work. Double orphan sweep (initDb + _restoreFromDb) is harmless redundancy.
